title_en: Prophethood and the Imamate in Al-Irshad
content_en:
Like most classical works of kalam theology, Al-Irshad devotes substantial attention to two interconnected topics: the rational and scriptural bases of prophethood, and the political and institutional question of the Imamate. Al-Juwayni's treatment of both topics is representative of the mainstream Ash'ari Shafi'i position and influenced subsequent discussions in the tradition.

On prophethood, al-Juwayni follows the standard Ash'ari framework: prophethood is rationally possible, its occurrence is confirmed by the performance of miracles that ordinary human beings cannot replicate, and the specific prophethood of Muhammad is established by the miracle of the Quran and by the historical record of his life and mission. He engages in detail with the epistemological questions involved in confirming prophetic miracles: how many witnesses are needed, what conditions must be met for a report of a miracle to constitute certain knowledge, and how the chain of transmission from witnesses to later generations is evaluated.

Al-Juwayni's discussion of mutawatir (mass-transmitted) reports is particularly important. He argues that reports transmitted by so many independent sources that coordinated deception is rationally impossible produce certain knowledge in recipients — not merely probable belief. The mass transmission of reports about the Prophet's life, his miracles, and the revelation of the Quran thus provides a rational basis for certain knowledge of prophetic authenticity that extends to those who did not personally witness the events.

On the Imamate, al-Juwayni wrote extensively both in Al-Irshad and in his more detailed political theology work Al-Ghiyathi. He defends the Sunni position against Shia claims for Ali's designated succession and argues that the caliphate of Abu Bakr was legitimate and had the community's consent. He also engages with the practical questions that had become urgent in his time: what qualifications are necessary for a valid Imam, what happens when fully qualified candidates are unavailable, and how the relationship between political and religious authority should be understood.

Al-Juwayni's political theology is notable for its realism and its willingness to adapt classical positions to contemporary circumstances. Writing in a period when the Seljuk sultans exercised the actual power of governance while Abbasid caliphs served a primarily symbolic function, he developed arguments for the legitimacy of governance by those with actual political capacity even when they did not meet all the classical criteria for the Imamate. This pragmatic adaptation influenced al-Ghazali's political theology and, through him, subsequent Sunni political thought.
content_id:
Bagian akhir Al-Irshad membahas tiga topik penting: pembuktian kenabian Muhammad, epistemologi hadis mutawatir, dan persoalan Imamah (kepemimpinan politik umat Islam).

Dalam membuktikan kenabian Muhammad, al-Juwayni mengikuti argumen standar teologi kalam: mukjizat adalah bukti kenabian yang tidak dapat dipungkiri. Mukjizat terbesar Nabi Muhammad adalah Al-Quran — sebuah kitab yang melampaui kemampuan seluruh manusia dan jin untuk menandinginya (i'jaz al-Quran). Al-Juwayni menjelaskan bahwa i'jaz Al-Quran bukan sekadar keindahan bahasanya, tetapi juga mencakup kandungannya, informasi gaib yang disampaikannya, dan pengaruhnya yang luar biasa terhadap kehidupan manusia.

Persoalan hadis mutawatir mendapat pembahasan mendalam dalam Al-Irshad. Hadis mutawatir adalah hadis yang diriwayatkan oleh begitu banyak orang pada setiap generasi sehingga secara logis tidak mungkin mereka semua bersepakat untuk berdusta. Pengetahuan yang dihasilkan hadis mutawatir bersifat qath'i (pasti), setara dengan pengetahuan inderawi. Al-Juwayni membahas persyaratan-persyaratan tawatur dan bagaimana menentukan apakah suatu berita telah mencapai derajat mutawatir.

Adapun persoalan Imamah — yang merupakan salah satu sumber perpecahan terbesar dalam sejarah Islam — al-Juwayni mengambil posisi Asy'ariyyah moderat. Imamah hukumnya wajib bagi umat Islam, bukan berdasarkan akal semata, melainkan berdasarkan syariat. Khalifah yang sah adalah yang dipilih berdasarkan persetujuan dan bai'at, bukan yang ditunjuk secara nas seperti yang diklaim kaum Syiah. Al-Juwayni juga membahas syarat-syarat seorang Imam dan apa yang harus dilakukan umat Islam ketika menghadapi imam yang fasik atau lalim.

Warisan intelektual Al-Irshad sangat dalam. Karya ini menjadi salah satu teks rujukan utama dalam teologi kalam di madrasah-madrasah Islam selama berabad-abad. Melalui murid-muridnya, terutama al-Ghazali, pemikiran al-Juwayni terus hidup dan berkembang dalam tradisi keilmuan Islam hingga hari ini.

Membaca Al-Irshad memberikan pemahaman mendalam tentang bagaimana para ulama Islam bergelut dengan pertanyaan-pertanyaan filosofis yang paling fundamental, sambil tetap berpegang pada komitmen mereka terhadap wahyu dan tradisi keilmuan Islam yang otentik.
content_ar:
Like most classical works of kalam theology, Al-Irshad devotes substantial attention to two interconnected topics: the rational and scriptural bases of prophethood, and the political and institutional question of the Imamate. Al-Juwayni's treatment of both topics is representative of the mainstream Ash'ari Shafi'i position and influenced subsequent discussions in the tradition.

On prophethood, al-Juwayni follows the standard Ash'ari framework: prophethood is rationally possible, its occurrence is confirmed by the performance of miracles that ordinary human beings cannot replicate, and the specific prophethood of Muhammad is established by the miracle of the Quran and by the historical record of his life and mission. He engages in detail with the epistemological questions involved in confirming prophetic miracles: how many witnesses are needed, what conditions must be met for a report of a miracle to constitute certain knowledge, and how the chain of transmission from witnesses to later generations is evaluated.

Al-Juwayni's discussion of mutawatir (mass-transmitted) reports is particularly important. He argues that reports transmitted by so many independent sources that coordinated deception is rationally impossible produce certain knowledge in recipients — not merely probable belief. The mass transmission of reports about النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم's life, his miracles, and the revelation of the Quran thus provides a rational basis for certain knowledge of prophetic authenticity that extends to those who did not personally witness the events.

On the Imamate, al-Juwayni wrote extensively both in Al-Irshad and in his more detailed political theology work Al-Ghiyathi. He defends the Sunni position against Shia claims for Ali's designated succession and argues that the caliphate of Abu Bakr was legitimate and had the community's consent. He also engages with the practical questions that had become urgent in his time: what qualifications are necessary for a valid Imam, what happens when fully qualified candidates are unavailable, and how the relationship between political and religious authority should be understood.

Al-Juwayni's political theology is notable for its realism and its willingness to adapt classical positions to contemporary circumstances. Writing in a period when the Seljuk sultans exercised the actual power of governance while Abbasid caliphs served a primarily symbolic function, he developed arguments for the legitimacy of governance by those with actual political capacity even when they did not meet all the classical criteria for the Imamate. This pragmatic adaptation influenced al-Ghazali's political theology and, through him, subsequent Sunni political thought.
